why_them: Award-winning D&G Life Café of the Year 2021 with zero standalone website — Facebook-only presence. Community-owned and well-regarded, they would benefit enormously from a simple brochure site with opening times, menu, and event updates to reduce reliance on Facebook reach.

Family-run café celebrating 25 years in business with no standalone website — Facebook-only. Rated #2 in Wigtown on TripAdvisor with a 4.7 score, showing strong demand. A professional site with menu, hours, and contact would convert tourist footfall much more effectively.

Fully licensed restaurant in Scotland's Book Town with no standalone website — Facebook only. Serves lunches and evening meals; caters for private parties and events. Missing a website means they're invisible to tourists planning ahead and lose bookings to competitors.

Busy town-centre sandwich bar with a 4.7 Google rating and no website whatsoever — Facebook only. Located in Dumfries centre, highly accessible. A simple site with menu, daily specials integration, and Google Maps embed would improve lunchtime footfall significantly.

#1 rated restaurant in Moffat on TripAdvisor with 4.6 stars and no standalone website — Facebook only. Moffat is a busy tourist town; a professional website with menu and opening hours would capture tourist searches and reduce dependency on social media reach.

Family-run bar and restaurant open since 2016. Domain gallowaybarn.co.uk resolves to a parked/broken page — effectively no working website. Primary presence is Facebook only. Caters for weddings and private events, losing bookings without a proper web presence.

Independent restaurant run by chef Callum and wife Magda with no standalone website — relies on Facebook and a third-party Dojo booking link. Strong TripAdvisor presence. A professional site with their story, menu, and integrated booking would elevate their brand significantly.

Established town-centre café opposite Stranraer Castle Square with outdoor seating — no standalone website, Facebook only. Serves all-day breakfast, pizzas, and coffee. Tourist footfall town; a basic site with menu and location would be an easy win.

Established Lockerbie café serving dine-in, takeaway and delivery with no standalone website — Facebook only. Lockerbie is accessible from Nith Digital's base. A simple branded site with online ordering or click-to-call would immediately add commercial value.

Highly rated Kirkcudbright café (4.7 Google) using a free Wix subdomain site — no custom domain, no SSL guarantee, looks amateur. Ranked #6 on TripAdvisor. Strong social media following. A professional WordPress or Next.js site with booking and menu would transform their credibility.

Established village pub-hotel with a decade-old Joomla site built circa 2016 — no online booking, outdated JavaScript libraries (MooTools, ExtJS), CSS errors, and no clear mobile-first design. Great TripAdvisor reviews. Easy upgrade to a modern WordPress or Webflow site.

Hotel-restaurant rated 4.4 on TripAdvisor whose site runs on HTTP with no SSL certificate — browsers flag it as insecure, destroying trust and deterring bookings. Site appears very old. No online booking visible. Strong local reputation deserving a modern, secure website.

Award-winning Dumfries restaurant (Best Independent Eatery 2020) with an expired SSL certificate — visitors see browser security warnings before they even load the page. HTTP only, no booking system evident. Right in Dumfries, highly accessible. Clear quick-win with a secure rebuilt site.

location: Robert Burns Centre, Mill Road, Dumfries
why_them: Popular Dumfries restaurant at the Burns Centre — domain fails to resolve (DNS error), meaning the website is effectively dead. All traffic falls to Facebook or TripAdvisor. A well-known establishment that urgently needs a working site with booking, menu, and evening specials.

Contemporary hotel-restaurant with a 2016 custom-coded jQuery site running on HTTP (no SSL) — browser security warnings for visitors. No mobile-first framework, outdated analytics (UA Google Analytics). The hotel markets beer garden events, cocktails and live sport but the site fails to represent this.

Contemporary café with its own domain returning a 404 error — the website no longer loads at all. Active on Facebook and well-reviewed but invisible online. A fresh site replacing the broken one would immediately restore online credibility and menu visibility.

Restaurant-with-rooms near Lockerbie with a 2015–2018 WordPress site using Revolution Slider (deprecated), spam link injection in content, and an incorrect copyright year. Functional but badly maintained, carrying SEO and trust risks. Accessible location for face-to-face.

Community arts centre café on Squarespace with no online menu, no booking system, and limited commercial content. D&G Life Café of the Year finalist. The Squarespace site is generic and does nothing to help tourists find or plan a visit. A Nith Digital upgrade would add menu, booking and local SEO.

Town-centre Dumfries café with no standalone website — Facebook only. May still be trading (TripAdvisor shows recent activity). Central Dumfries location, directly accessible. If still open, a simple site with menu and hours would immediately increase footfall from search traffic.

Heritage hotel midway between Dumfries and Castle Douglas with a heritage/tourism positioning. Website present but the visual design and CMS appear dated with little evidence of modern responsive features, online booking or menu. Premium malt whisky and gin bar deserves a premium digital presence.
